title: Trump and Latin America Archives - Americas Quarterly
gist: An Americas Quarterly archive index of Trump-era Latin America coverage, clustering around a Venezuelan democratic/oil transition, the U.S. use of foreign-terrorist-organization designations against Brazilian gangs, tariff impacts, and questions over U.S. commitment to hemispheric institutions.

insights.claim: Recovering Venezuela's oil production is framed as unviable without institutional change—requiring sanctions relief, a democratic transition, and deep reforms together, not sanctions relief alone.
insights.speaker: Americas Quarterly
insights.so_what: Tempers expectations that a Maduro exit alone unlocks a Venezuelan oil bonanza—reform sequencing gates any production or investment thesis.
insights.locator: Without Institutional Change, Venezuela’s Oil Bonanza

insights.claim: Washington is described as having three distinct pathways to provide sanctions relief to Venezuela and PDVSA, with Syria, Sudan and Iraq offered as historical precedents for compliant corporate re-entry.
insights.speaker: Americas Quarterly
insights.so_what: Gives energy investors concrete regulatory templates for how U.S. firms could legally return to Venezuelan oil.
insights.locator: How U.S. Companies May Return to Venezuela

insights.claim: With Maduro removed, the U.S. is framed as facing the difficult task of limiting the influence of China, Russia and Iran in Venezuela without triggering instability.
insights.speaker: Americas Quarterly
insights.so_what: Great-power competition, not just governance, will shape post-Maduro Venezuela's asset ownership and reconstruction contracts.

insights.claim: A successful Venezuelan transition is presented as hinging on investment and on who leads the military, with the reactivation of the Democratic Unitary Platform and opposition figure Machado's return cited as the most delicate challenges.
insights.speaker: Americas Quarterly
insights.so_what: Military leadership and opposition cohesion are the pivot variables for any Venezuela transition timeline.

themes.theme: FTO designations as U.S. policy tool

insights.claim: A possible U.S. designation of Brazil's PCC and CV gangs as foreign terrorist organizations is framed as carrying consequences for banks, companies and politics.
insights.speaker: Americas Quarterly
insights.so_what: FTO designations would create material compliance and counterparty risk for banks and companies operating in Brazil.

insights.claim: New U.S. foreign-terrorist-organization designations are described as posing unprecedented compliance challenges for businesses operating in Brazil.
insights.speaker: Americas Quarterly
insights.so_what: Signals rising legal/compliance overhead for firms with Brazilian exposure under the Trump administration's designation strategy.
insights.locator: The New FTO Risks to Businesses in Brazil

themes.theme: Trade, tariffs and North American integration

insights.claim: Experts quoted argue Latin America remains well-positioned to absorb Trump's new 15% tariff move.
insights.speaker: Americas Quarterly
insights.so_what: Suggests limited regional macro damage from the 15% tariff—counter to a more alarmist consensus.

insights.claim: USMCA is argued likely to survive its review because preserving North American integration remains the least costly path forward despite political and economic tests.
insights.speaker: Americas Quarterly
insights.so_what: Leans against the trade-fracture narrative—supportive for North American cross-border supply chains and Mexican assets.
insights.locator: Why USMCA May Survive After All

insights.claim: Washington is described as threatening to walk away from the OAS, framed as one of the hemisphere's most essential institutions and an existential challenge for the body.
insights.speaker: Americas Quarterly
insights.so_what: A U.S. exit would weaken multilateral hemispheric governance and shift dispute resolution toward bilateral leverage.

insights.claim: Negotiating with Cuba's armed forces is framed as possibly the only viable path to change on the island, despite discomfort it would cause.
insights.speaker: Americas Quarterly
insights.so_what: Points to the Cuban military—not the party—as the real counterparty for any U.S.-Cuba opening.
insights.locator: Cuba’s Military: The Institution Washington Cannot Ignore
